notes: Picked a good handful of blueberries after hiking to the top of the beehive trail. Hiked Cadillac Mountain in reverse: parking at the top, then hiking across to Dorr Mountain and back.
The Old House at Peace field contains John Adams standing desk, which he used throughout a large part of his career. The inner compartments of the desk are still labeled in his own handwriting.
The 419 skeletal remains (average age of 22 years old) uncovered during the construction of a federal building were reinterred at the memorial site.

We took a tour with Jolene Babyak, who lived on Alacatraz as a child while her father was a guard and, later, assistant warden at the prison. The prison held up to 250 inmates, but the island was also home to over 60 families, including 75 children who took the ferry to and from school every day.

The Battle of Antietam on September 17, 1862 remains the bloodiest single day in American history, with about 23,000 killed, wounded, or missing in roughly 12 hours, and it led Lincoln to issue the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation five days later.
The parkway opened in October 1954; on the ~19-mile section the NPS administers, commercial trucks and vehicles over 10,000 pounds are banned.
Visited Slater Mill, the site of the first water-powered textile mill in the United States.

The site's African Meeting House, built in 1806, is the oldest surviving Black church building in the U.S.; it hosted abolitionists like Frederick Douglass and William Lloyd Garrison and was nicknamed the "Black Faneuil Hall."
Collected a variety of sea glass from Spectacle Island.

Visited all sites, starting with a climb to the top of the Bunker Hill Monument, then the USS Constitution and USS Casin Young at the Charlestown Navy Yard, ending at Faneuil Hall. Favorite Fact: The construction of Dry Dock 1 at the Navy yard in 1833, along with the construction of a second dry dock in Norfolk, VA, was the largest civil works project undertaken by the United States at the time.
Favorite Fact: Castle Clinton was the location of the New York Aquarium from 1896 to 1941.
Visited and transmitted from the Marconi wireless station. Collected a good amount of driftwood, which was boiled and is now in aquariums.

Protects 24 miles of undeveloped barrier-island beach, the longest stretch of wild Atlantic coast in Florida. Home to Turtle Mound, a roughly 50-foot Timucua shell midden built up over centuries of discarded oyster shells.
The canal runs 184.5 miles from Georgetown to Cumberland, using 74 lift locks to overcome the 605-foot elevation change along the route.

Established in 1974, this was the first NPS site dedicated to a woman; the Glen Echo house served as an American Red Cross warehouse from 1891 and as its national headquarters from 1897 to 1904, as well as Barton's home until her death in 1912.
On a small island reached by a wooden footbridge, 56 granite blocks carry the signature, hometown, and occupation of each signer of the Declaration of Independence; dedicated July 2, 1984.
Designed by Frank Gehry and dedicated in 2020, it features a 447-foot-long stainless steel tapestry, the largest ever attempted, depicting the Pointe du Hoc cliffs of Normandy in peacetime.
A reading room containing all of Edgar Allen Poe's works had been built within the house, and many of the visitors spent some time sitting there reading some of his stories.
The Ellis Island Museum was the most extensive NPS museum we have visited. The exhibits of the screening process and family heirlooms were of particular interest.
Eleanor Roosevelt moved to this property after the death of FDR.

Eugene O'Neill is the only American playwright to win a Nobel Prize for Literature. He had extremely fond memories of his time as a sailor, and specially designed his office with a nautical theme reminiscent of the officer's quarters.
Federal Hall was the site of many historic moments including the Zenger trial (helping to establish freedom of the press), authoring of the Bill of Rights, and the inauguration of George Washington.
The Fire Island lighthouse was originally constructed at the west end of the island, but is currently more than 5 miles east of the island's end, due to the island's growth from littoral drift.

The fort's defense during the Battle of Baltimore on September 13–14, 1814 inspired Francis Scott Key to write "The Star-Spangled Banner"; it is the only place designated both a National Monument and a Historic Shrine.

Fort Point was used for various military purposes beginning during the Civil War and ending with WWII. It is located underneath the Golden Gate Bridge, which provides an interesting view of the bridge's structure from below.
Spread over 7.5 acres, the memorial is laid out as four open-air "rooms," one for each of FDR's four terms, with 21 of his quotations carved in granite.

Favorite Feature: The drafting rooms on the top floor of the house contained a number of tools for the creation of blueprints, including drafting tables on tracks that could be pushed outdoors to expose in the sun, a floor-to-ceiling glass cylinder lit from the center around which blueprints could be wrapped, and a closet that was retrofitted as an arsenic gas chamber for exposing blueprints chemically.
Delaware ratified the Constitution by a unanimous 30–0 vote on December 7, 1787, making it the first state; established in 2014, this was the first National Park System unit located within Delaware.
The mausoleum for President Grant and his wife, Julia, is the largest in North America.

Fought July 1–3, 1863, it was the Civil War's bloodiest battle with about 51,000 casualties; the repulse of Pickett's Charge at the "High Water Mark" marked the farthest Confederate advance into the North.

Gloria Dei is Pennsylvania's oldest church, and still has an active congregation. It was founded by Swedish Lutherans, and has many items inside that were brought over by the original Swedish missionaries, including a baptismal font and hand-carved wooden angels.

Opened in 1891 as a National Chautauqua Assembly before becoming the area's premier amusement park; its 1921 Dentzel carousel, with 39 horses plus hand-carved menagerie animals, still operates seasonally.

The chief architect of the Golden Gate Bridge, Joseph Strauss, was one of the first to strictly apply hard hat and safety harness rules during work on the project; a safety net used during the construction of the bridge's roadway saved the lives of 19 workers.

We visited both Fort Jay and Castle Williams during the visit. There was a 'Roaring Twenties' themed event happening while we were there, so there were a few hundred people dressed up in period clothes. Holum and Sumanth took out the ferry to meet us there.

The Patowmack Canal here, built 1785–1802 under the leadership of George Washington as first president of the Patowmack Company, was among the first canal systems in the U.S. and let boats skirt the Great Falls of the Potomac.
Its southern section began as the Mount Vernon Memorial Highway, which opened in 1932 for the bicentennial of Washington's birth and was the first portion of what became the parkway.

At the confluence of the Shenandoah and Potomac, it was the target of abolitionist John Brown's 1859 raid on the federal armory. Storer College, founded here in 1867, was among the first integrated schools in the U.S. and hosted the Niagara Movement's first American meeting in 1906.

The home has been moved twice: once to accommodate the introduction of the New York City street grid plan, and once again to move it to a larger lot. In the second move, the house needed to be lifted over thirty feet in the air with hydraulic lifts, as it needed to pass over the roof of a church.

When completed around 1790, the ~24,000-square-foot Hampton Mansion was believed to be the largest private home in the U.S.; in 1948 it became the first site named a National Historic Site for its architectural significance.

These geometric earthworks were built by Native Americans of the Hopewell culture roughly 1,600 to 2,000 years ago; in September 2023 the Hopewell Ceremonial Earthworks became the nation's 25th UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The foyer of FDR's house was decorated as it was when he lived in the home. It contained a glass display case containing songbirds and birds of prey that Roosevelt shot and taxidermied as a child.

Hopewell Furnace began operations more than one hundred years after the first ironworks were founded in Saugus, Massachusetts. The smelting process and construction of the forges was virtually identical, except for Hopewell's larger overall scale.

In addition to the Liberty Bell and Independence Hall, this site also had other artifacts on display including the Syng inkstand, which was used by many of the signers of both the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.
We started out along lake Michigan and hiked across the dunes. The area was particularly busy at that time of year because of the beach.

New Orleans Jazz is one of the few national parks dedicated to a musical art form. Its live performances are held at the Old U.S. Mint, the only U.S. Mint to have struck coinage under three governments: the United States, the State of Louisiana, and the Confederacy.

Favorite Feature: The entrance to each tram car is only 4.5 feet tall! Such small trams are necessary due to the arch narrowing to less than 7 feet at the top. The tram cars also rotate 155 degrees over the course of the ride in order to keep passengers upright.

Jean Lafitte is not a single park but six separate sites spread across southern Louisiana. Its namesake, the pirate and privateer Jean Lafitte, helped General Andrew Jackson defend the city at the 1815 Battle of New Orleans in exchange for pardons for himself and his men.

The Kennedy family sold the property in 1920, and it remained a private residence until 1966 when Rose Kennedy repurchased the house. About 20% of the items in the house were original to the Kennedy family, including the bassinet in the nursery that was used by all of the Kennedy children.

John Muir inherited this 10,000 square foot house and orchard from his father in law. The National Park Service still maintains some a small orchard, and we were able to take home lemons as souvenirs!
The 19 stainless steel soldier statues are doubled by their reflection in the granite wall to form 38 figures, referencing both the 38th Parallel and the war's 38 months.
The trail follows roughly 4,900 miles of the Corps of Discovery's 1804–1806 route across 16 states, winding through the homelands of more than 60 Tribal nations.

Favorite Feature: Lincoln's bedroom contains his original writing desk, which was very plain and small. It held sentimental value for him as he had purchased the desk early in his career, so he kept and used it long after he could have afforded a more ornate desk, to the chagrin of Mary Todd.
The seated marble Lincoln stands 19 feet tall and was carved from 28 interlocking blocks of Georgia white marble by the Piccirilli Brothers.

The primary tenement building was being restored during our visit, so we saw apartments in the building next door. One of the interesting items from the period were the natural gas canisters that were used by the stove. Prior to the installation of gas lines and meters, the canister would be filled periodically, and the resident would deposit a coin into a slot, which would allow a predetermined amount of gas to be let out.

Favorite Fact: The property was purchased by Nathaniel Appleton as a gift to his daughter and son-in-law, Henry Wadsworth Longfellow. The Appleton family also owned the mill factories that grew into the city of Lowell.
Favorite Fact: The factories of the city were operated by a series of canals that are almost six miles long. This remains the largest series of power canals in the United States.

The First Battle of Bull Run on July 21, 1861 was the first major land battle of the Civil War; Thomas J. Jackson earned the nickname "Stonewall" here when General Bee rallied his men by shouting that Jackson stood "like a stone wall."
Walked the length of Battle Road.

Opened in 2011, it was the first Mall memorial to honor an African American and the first to honor a citizen activist rather than a president or war hero; visitors pass through the split "Mountain of Despair" to reach the "Stone of Hope."

Morristown served as the site of Washington's headquarters, as well as the Continental Army's winter encampment during 1780, which happened to be the coldest winter on record. The constructed a planned settlement consisting of 14x16 foot log cabins.

Hiked the Dipsea Trail to the Ben Johnson Trail to the Hillside trail, ending in Cathedral Grove. Despite full parking lots, we encountered almost no other hikers until arriving back at the paved section through the grove.
Designated by Congress in 1965, it preserves over 1,000 acres, dating to George Washington's 1790 selection of the site along the Potomac.
Favorite Fact: The last whaling ship left New Bedford harbor in 1925.
Not a single park but an administrative office overseeing 10+ NPS sites around the harbor, including the Statue of Liberty, Ellis Island, and Governors Island.
"America's Main Street" was laid out by Pierre L'Enfant to physically connect the White House and the U.S. Capitol, linking the executive and legislative branches.

Pinnacles is a relatively small park and we were able to hike all of the trails in two days. The park has interesting geological features, as well as some interesting trails (some lead through caves, others required mining short tunnels through rock faces that could not be climbed around). On our second morning, we were able to watch a group of condors from a distance.
We walked out to Chimney Rock and to Abbots Lagoon during our visit. There were a significant number of valley quail visible from the trails or sitting on fence posts alongside the road.

The Presidio was transferred to the National Park Service in 1994. Prior to that, it had been an active military base for over two hundred years, spanning three countries. At the time the Presidio was transferred, it was the oldest continually operational military base in the country.
The museum featured a replica of a Pullman sleeper car, including a bed that folded down from the overhead compartment.

Authorized by Congress and signed by President Benjamin Harrison in 1890, it was the third national park established by the federal government, after Yellowstone and Mackinac (the latter is no longer a national park).

The site was primarily a museum describing the development of Richmond, CA and the shipyards needed to support the war effort. We visited the SS Red Oak Victory, which was docked nearby. It is the last remaining WWII ship built at the Kaiser Shipyards to survive.

Located in the center of Providence, the memorial is located directly across the street from Roger Williams house. He settled in this location due to the proximity to an active freshwater spring. The spring is located within the memorial.

There are a number of historic ships docked at the Hyde Street pier that are open for visitors. The pier (and the ferries that departed from it) were part of the 101 highway until the construction of the Golden Gate Bridge was completed.

Visited on our way back from vacation in Vermont. Notable statues were the Standing Lincoln and a cast of the Massachusetts 54th Regiment Memorial. Flushed a woodcock while walking through the garden.
The uniforms Theodore Roosevelt wore while serving as a Colonel in the Rough Riders were tailored by Brooks Brothers.

Favorite Fact: The site was rediscovered and excavated by Roland W. Robbins, an amateur archeologist. Robbins is also responsible for discovering the foundation of Thoreau's house at Walden Pond, as well as John Alden's home in Duxbury.
Favorite Fact: The Salem Maritime NHP was the first National Historic Site established by the Park Service.

The contested 1733 Eastchester election held on the village green here was reported in the first issue of John Peter Zenger's New-York Weekly Journal, the paper whose 1735 libel acquittal helped establish freedom of the press in America.
The museum has a display of government issued firearms that suffered unusual damage, including lightning strikes and barrels split by oncoming bullets.

Favorite Fact: The earliest settlers of Ste. Genevieve began farming in the fertile soil along the Mississippi River before establishing a permanent settlement. They would travel to the fields in the spring to plant crops and return only in the fall to harvest.
Favorite Fact: The copper sheeting that makes up the Statue of Liberty's form is only as thick as only two pennies!
Designated by President Obama in 2016, it was the first national monument dedicated to LGBTQ history and rights; it preserves the site of the uprising that began June 28, 1969.

In the 1930s landscape architects transformed the former Mason's Island into a "real forest" mimicking the native woodland; its memorial centers on a 17-foot bronze statue of Roosevelt by Paul Manship.
The 19-foot statue of Jefferson was originally installed in 1943 as a plaster cast due to wartime metal restrictions, and was not replaced with the bronze version until 1947.
We visited the home where Thaddeus Kosciuszko stayed during the period he lived in Pennsylvania after the Revolutionary War. This is the smallest unit maintained by the NPS.

The Roosevelt family moved out of the property in 1872, and in 1916 the building was demolished. Members of the Roosevelt family re-purchased the lot and reconstructed the family home in the same style as the original. There is an attached museum, which contains the items Roosevelt was wearing and carrying when he was shot while campaigning, including the folded up speech and glasses case that slowed the trajectory of the bullet.

Dedicated in 1763, it is the oldest surviving synagogue building in the U.S. and the recipient of George Washington's 1790 letter affirming religious liberty, pledging that the government "gives to bigotry no sanction, to persecution no assistance."

Favorite Fact: Grant's residence, Whitehaven, was painted a shade of green known as Paris Green. The color was so named as it contained arsenic and was once used to kill rats in the sewers of Paris. Interestingly, there is also a green dress in the Lincoln home that requires special care when being handled since it also contains traces of arsenic.

No battle was fought here, yet roughly 2,000 of the 12,000 Continental soldiers who wintered in 1777–1778 died of disease; Baron von Steuben arrived in February 1778 and drilled the army into a disciplined fighting force.

While the mansion cost $660,000 to build and $1.5 million to furnish, no buyers were willing to purchase the property for more than a few hundred thousand dollars during the Great Depression. As a result, the property was donated to the Park Service in exchange for a significant tax deduction instead.
Designed by Maya Lin, a 21-year-old Yale undergraduate, whose entry beat more than 1,400 others in a blind competition; the design had earned only a B in her architecture class.

The obelisk is visibly two-toned because construction stalled for over 20 years; when work resumed in the 1870s the marble came from different quarries and has weathered to noticeably different colors.
There were a number of interesting items from the Weir family on display in their home, including a taxidermied Cooper's Hawk.
The White House contains 132 rooms, 35 bathrooms, and 8 staircases across 6 levels, plus 412 doors, 147 windows, and 28 fireplaces.

Established by Congress in 1966 on land donated by Catherine Filene Shouse, Wolf Trap is the only national park dedicated to the performing arts; its Filene Center amphitheater seats roughly 7,000 under cover and on the lawn.
The Freedom Wall holds 4,048 gold stars, each representing roughly 100 Americans who died in the war.
Opened in 2021 at Pershing Park, it is Washington's newest war memorial; its centerpiece, Sabin Howard's 58-foot bronze relief "A Soldier's Journey," was installed in September 2024.

We stayed in Curry Village in Yosemite valley, which helped us get an early start before the park got too busy. During our first full day in the park, we hiked to the top of Yosemite falls. The upper Yosemite falls trail is one of Yosemite's oldest trails; construction started in 1873. On our second day, we were able to hike through the sequoias in Mariposa grove.
